insights: Collaborative goals achieve a 6.56 percentage point higher success rate (27.35% vs 20.79%) than non-collaborative goals, but the impact varies dramatically by department: HR gains +23.09 pp while Marketing suffers a -6.61 pp decline, indicating collaboration is not universally beneficial and requires department-specific governance.

Q4 delivers the highest goal success rate (31.82%), outperforming Q3—the lowest quarter—by 17.48 pp; November-starting goals achieve the peak success rate (36.78%), exceeding August-starting goals by 24.14 pp, revealing that timing—not just quarter—is a dominant operational lever.

High priority goals consistently outperform all other priority levels across all five categories, with success rates ranging from 23.21% (Innovation) to 39.66% (Customer Satisfaction), while Low priority goals in Process Improvement achieve only 3.23% success—highlighting a 36.43 pp performance gap that signals severe resource misalignment for low-priority, high-effort categories.

The optimal priority-category combination is High + Customer Satisfaction (39.66% success, n=58), whereas Low + Process Improvement is the worst (3.23% success, n=31), and High priority is the top performer in every category—confirming that elevating priority is the most universally effective lever for improving goal outcomes.

Department-level seasonal patterns diverge sharply: IT and Finance both peak in Q4 (38.00% and 34.55%), HR peaks in Q2 (31.48%), and Marketing shows no clear quarterly pattern—indicating that centralized seasonal planning must be calibrated per department to avoid under-resourcing high-opportunity windows.

Critical priority goals underperform High priority goals by 21.46 pp overall (13.19% vs 34.64%), yet Critical goals in Cost Reduction achieve 16.67% success—nearly double their average—suggesting that priority labels may be inconsistently applied or misaligned with actual strategic weight in certain categories.
summary: The analysis directly addresses the user’s core goals: cross-departmental collaboration delivers a net 6.56 pp success lift but with strong departmental heterogeneity—HR benefits massively while Marketing is harmed; seasonal timing exerts even stronger influence, with November starts achieving 36.78% success versus 12.64% for August starts, and Q4 outperforming Q3 by 17.48 pp; and priority-category alignment proves decisive—High priority is superior in all categories, with Customer Satisfaction benefiting most (39.66%) and Process Improvement suffering most when deprioritized (3.23%). These findings reveal that strategic goal execution is less about uniform policy and more about context-aware calibration: prioritizing High-priority goals launched in Q4 or November, especially in Customer Satisfaction or Cost Reduction, while re-evaluating collaboration mandates in Marketing and deprioritization logic in Process Improvement. The next focus should be diagnosing why Marketing’s collaboration efforts reduce success and validating whether Low-priority Process Improvement goals are chronically under-resourced or fundamentally mis-scoped.
